quiz_questions.question_text: Earth completes one full rotation on its axis in approximately how many hours?
quiz_questions.explanation: Earth rotates once on its axis approximately every 24 hours, giving us the cycle of day and night.

quiz_questions.question_text: Earth's revolution around the Sun takes approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Earth takes about 365.25 days to complete one orbit around the Sun, which is why we have leap years.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the approximate tilt of Earth's axis?
quiz_questions.explanation: Earth's axis is tilted about 23.5° relative to its orbital plane, which causes the seasons.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sidereal day is approximately how long?
quiz_questions.explanation: A sidereal day (one full rotation relative to distant stars) is about 23 hours and 56 minutes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which effect causes moving objects on Earth's surface to deflect from a straight path?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Coriolis effect, caused by Earth's rotation, deflects moving objects to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere.

quiz_questions.question_text: Perihelion is when Earth is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Perihelion occurs around January 3, when Earth is closest to the Sun in its elliptical orbit.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the shape of Earth's orbit around the Sun?
quiz_questions.explanation: Earth's orbit is nearly circular with an eccentricity of about 0.017, making it only slightly elliptical.

quiz_questions.question_text: The plane of Earth's orbit around the Sun is called the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The ecliptic plane is the plane of Earth's orbit around the Sun.

quiz_questions.question_text: What demonstrated that Earth rotates on its axis?
quiz_questions.explanation: Léon Foucault's pendulum experiment in 1851 provided visible proof that Earth rotates.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do we have leap years?

options.text: Earth's orbital period is about 365.25 days

quiz_questions.explanation: Since Earth's orbital period is approximately 365.25 days, we add an extra day every 4 years to keep the calendar aligned.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'rotation' in the context of Earth's motion?

options.text: The spinning of Earth on its axis

quiz_questions.explanation: Rotation refers to Earth spinning on its own axis, which takes about 24 hours and produces day and night.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'revolution' in astronomy?

options.text: The spinning of a planet on its axis
The orbital motion of one body around another

quiz_questions.explanation: Revolution is the movement of one celestial body in an orbit around another, such as Earth orbiting the Sun.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'sidereal day'?

options.text: The time for one orbit around the Sun
The time for one full rotation relative to distant stars
The time between two consecutive noons

quiz_questions.explanation: A sidereal day is measured by Earth's rotation relative to distant stars, about 23 hours 56 minutes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'ecliptic'?

options.text: The apparent path of the Sun across the sky over a year
The shadow cast during an eclipse

quiz_questions.explanation: The ecliptic is the apparent path the Sun traces across the sky throughout the year, corresponding to Earth's orbital plane.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'precession'?

options.text: The tilting of the Moon's orbit
The slow wobble of Earth's rotational axis
The speeding up of Earth's rotation

quiz_questions.explanation: Precession is the slow, conical wobble of Earth's axis over a ~26,000-year cycle, changing which star is the 'North Star.'

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'perihelion' mean?

options.text: The point in Earth's orbit nearest to the Sun
The point farthest from the Sun

quiz_questions.explanation: Perihelion comes from Greek 'peri' (near) + 'helios' (sun), marking Earth's closest approach to the Sun.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'aphelion' mean?

options.text: The point in Earth's orbit farthest from the Sun
The point closest to the Sun
The highest point in the atmosphere

quiz_questions.explanation: Aphelion is when Earth is at its greatest distance from the Sun, occurring around July 4.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'Coriolis effect'?

options.text: The bending of light near a black hole
The deflection of moving objects due to Earth's rotation
The pull of the Moon on tides

quiz_questions.explanation: The Coriolis effect is an apparent deflection of moving objects caused by Earth's rotation, influencing weather patterns and ocean currents.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'celestial equator'?

options.text: The projection of Earth's equator onto the celestial sphere
A line connecting the poles of the galaxy

quiz_questions.explanation: The celestial equator is the imaginary great circle on the celestial sphere directly above Earth's equator.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'analemma'?

options.text: A figure-8 pattern showing the Sun's position at the same time each day over a year

quiz_questions.explanation: An analemma is the figure-8 shape traced by the Sun's position in the sky when observed at the same time and place throughout the year.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that shadows are shorter at noon in June than in December (in the Northern Hemisphere). What best explains this?

options.text: Earth's axial tilt causes the Sun to appear higher in summer
Earth is closer to the Sun in June
The Moon blocks some sunlight in winter
The atmosphere is thinner in summer

quiz_questions.explanation: Earth's 23.5° axial tilt causes the Sun to reach a higher angle in the sky during summer, producing shorter shadows.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronaut watches Earth from space and sees the terminator (day-night boundary). Over 24 hours, how does the terminator appear to move?

options.text: It moves from pole to pole
It sweeps westward across Earth's surface

quiz_questions.explanation: As Earth rotates eastward, the day-night line (terminator) appears to sweep westward across the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pilot flies directly north from the equator. Due to Earth's rotation, the plane drifts to the east of its intended path. What effect explains this?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Coriolis effect deflects moving objects to the right in the Northern Hemisphere (and left in the Southern), causing the drift.

quiz_questions.question_text: If Earth's axial tilt were 0°, what would happen to seasons?

options.text: There would be no seasons — temperature would depend only on latitude
Only the equator would have seasons

quiz_questions.explanation: Without axial tilt, sunlight distribution would not change throughout the year, eliminating seasons.

quiz_questions.question_text: A calendar shows February 29. What astronomical fact makes this extra day necessary?

options.text: Mars comes close to Earth every 4 years
Earth's orbital period is approximately 365.25 days, not exactly 365
The Moon's orbit slows down every 4 years

quiz_questions.explanation: The extra ~0.25 day per year accumulates, and adding a leap day every 4 years keeps the calendar synchronized with Earth's orbit.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student sets up a Foucault pendulum at the North Pole. How would it appear to behave?

options.text: The pendulum would not appear to rotate at all
The pendulum's plane of swing would rotate 360° in 24 hours
The pendulum would rotate 180° in 24 hours
The pendulum would stop swinging quickly

quiz_questions.explanation: At the North Pole, a Foucault pendulum's swing plane appears to rotate a full 360° in ~24 hours due to Earth's rotation beneath it.

quiz_questions.question_text: Astronomers measure that a star returns to the same position in the sky 4 minutes earlier each night. Why?

options.text: The difference between solar and sidereal day — Earth moves along its orbit
The star is moving toward Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: Because Earth advances ~1° along its orbit each day, stars rise ~4 minutes earlier each night (the sidereal vs. solar day difference).

quiz_questions.question_text: If Earth's orbit were perfectly circular instead of slightly elliptical, what would change?

options.text: There would be no perihelion or aphelion — Earth-Sun distance would be constant

quiz_questions.explanation: With a circular orbit, Earth's distance from the Sun would never vary, eliminating perihelion and aphelion.

quiz_questions.question_text: A photographer takes a picture of the Sun at the same time every day for a year and notices a figure-8 pattern. What is this called?
quiz_questions.explanation: The figure-8 pattern formed by the Sun's position at the same time each day over a year is called an analemma.

quiz_questions.question_text: Scientists predict that in about 13,000 years, the North Celestial Pole will point near the star Vega instead of Polaris. What causes this shift?

options.text: The Sun moving through the galaxy

quiz_questions.explanation: Precession causes Earth's axis to trace a circle in the sky over ~26,000 years, so in ~13,000 years the axis will point near Vega.
